evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: I want to build a todo app with a React frontend and a REST API backend.
expectations: Asks questions rather than making statements or providing answers
Covers multiple decision branches — not just technology choices but also scope, users, or deployment
Asks about data persistence or storage approach
Asks about authentication or who the users are
Does not make implementation decisions for the user or recommend a specific stack unprompted

evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: We're going to add a Redis caching layer in front of our main PostgreSQL database to improve performance.
expectations: Asks questions rather than endorsing or rejecting the approach
Asks about cache invalidation strategy or TTL decisions
Asks about what data will be cached and why those queries are slow
Asks about consistency requirements or acceptable staleness
Covers operational concerns — monitoring, cache warming, fallback behavior, or failure modes

evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: We're going to version our API using URL path versioning like /v1/, /v2/ going forward.
expectations: Asks questions rather than approving or rejecting the versioning strategy
Asks about existing clients or how breaking changes will be communicated
Asks about deprecation policy — how long old versions are supported
Covers more than just the URL scheme — asks about what triggers a version bump or how divergence between versions is managed
Does not lecture about REST versioning best practices without being asked

evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: We want to migrate our monolith to microservices over the next 6 months.
expectations: Asks questions rather than validating or dismissing the migration plan
Asks about the motivation or specific pain point driving the migration
Asks about team structure — who owns each service, how many engineers are involved
Asks about data ownership or how shared databases will be handled
Covers deployment and operational concerns — service discovery, distributed tracing, or rollback strategy

evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: We're replacing our homegrown session-based auth with Auth0 for all our apps.
expectations: Asks questions rather than endorsing or warning against the decision
Asks about the migration path for existing users or active sessions
Asks about which apps are in scope and whether they share the same user base
Covers risk and rollback — what happens if Auth0 is unavailable or the migration goes wrong
Does not assume the decision is final — probes whether alternatives were considered

evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: We're going to shard our user database by user ID to handle our growing dataset.
expectations: Asks questions rather than endorsing or warning against sharding
Asks about the actual scale problem — current data size, query patterns, or what is actually slow
Asks about cross-shard queries or operations that span multiple users
Asks about the sharding key choice and whether user ID distribution is uniform
Covers operational overhead — re-sharding, hotspots, or whether simpler alternatives were considered

evals.prompt: Grill me on this plan: We want to redesign our new hire onboarding process. The plan is a 30-day structured program with weekly check-ins.
expectations: Asks questions rather than endorsing the 30-day structure
Asks about the specific problems with the current onboarding — what prompted this redesign
Asks about who owns the program and who runs the weekly check-ins
Covers success metrics — how will the team know if the new process is better
Asks at least one question about edge cases or roles — whether all new hires follow the same program regardless of role or seniority

evals.prompt: I want to implement a feature flag system in-house instead of using LaunchDarkly or Split. The plan: store flags in a Redis hash, read them on each request with a thin SDK wrapper, and use our existing admin panel to toggle flags. We have about 30 engineers and 15 services. Grill me on this.
evals.expected_output: A relentless interrogation covering build-vs-buy reasoning, failure modes, missing capabilities, and operational readiness — framed as questions, not analysis.
expectations: At least one question directly probes why building in-house is preferred — asking about cost, missing features in commercial tools, or what was evaluated and rejected
At least one question asks about a failure scenario: Redis downtime, stale flags, fallback defaults, or bad flag rollout detection
At least one question probes capabilities the plan omits: user targeting, percentage rollouts, audit logging, or gradual rollout strategies

The response is predominantly questions. It does not include extended multi-sentence analysis, explanations, or answers to its own questions after posing them. Brief framing is acceptable but the majority of content must be questions awaiting the user's input.
The response does not include a summary judgment, bottom-line evaluation, or prescriptive conclusion section that evaluates the plan or tells the user what to do next.

evals.prompt: We're migrating our CI/CD from Jenkins to GitHub Actions. Plan: rewrite all 40 Jenkinsfiles as GHA workflows over 3 months, run both in parallel during migration, then decommission Jenkins. We have a platform team of 3 who will do all the rewrites. Grill me.
evals.expected_output: Deep interrogation covering migration sequencing, team capacity realism, verification parity, and operational concerns — all as questions, no checklists or advice.
expectations: At least one question asks how the 40 pipelines will be prioritized, sequenced, or categorized for migration — not just 'will you migrate them all at once'
At least one question probes whether 3 engineers can realistically handle 40 pipelines in 3 months, or how other responsibilities are handled during this period
At least one question asks how the team will verify that new GHA workflows produce equivalent results to Jenkins pipelines during parallel run
The response does not contain imperative recommendations ('you should', 'make sure to', 'I recommend', 'consider using'). All concerns are framed as questions, not advice.

Questions do not immediately answer themselves. A question like 'Have you done the math? That's 13 per person, which sounds fine until...' fails because it provides the analysis. Questions should wait for the user's response.

evals.prompt: We're going to improve our deployment process.
evals.expected_output: Refuses to accept the vagueness. Fires back with probing questions across multiple dimensions, without endorsing the goal or promising solutions.
expectations: At least one question asks about what the current deployment process looks like today
At least one question probes what 'improve' means — what's broken, what the pain points are, or what success looks like
The response does not endorse or encourage the goal with phrases like 'worthwhile', 'great idea', 'sounds good', or 'happy to help'
At least one question probes what triggered this — was there an incident, a complaint, a scaling problem, or an external pressure that prompted this now
The response does not promise to provide suggestions, solutions, or recommendations. It stays in interrogation mode.

evals.prompt: I'm opening a second location for my bakery. The first one has been profitable for 3 years. I'm planning to hire a manager for the new location, replicate the same menu, and take out a small business loan to cover the buildout. The new location is in a neighborhood about 20 minutes away that has a lot of foot traffic. Grill me on this.
evals.expected_output: Probing questions about staffing risk, financial exposure, market fit, operational complexity — specific to the bakery details given, with no congratulations or generic advice.
expectations: At least one question asks about financial risk — loan terms, what happens if the second location underperforms, break-even timeline, or impact on the first location
At least one question asks about how operations will work across two locations — quality consistency, supply chain, the owner's time split, or management oversight
The response does not congratulate the user, call the plan 'exciting', 'great', or 'a good idea', or otherwise endorse the decision before probing it

The response is predominantly questions. It does not include extended analysis like 'Bakeries are notoriously thin — 5-10% net is common' or provide answers alongside questions. Questions should wait for the user's input.

Questions do not telegraph the expected answer. 'What are your actual margins?' is open-ended. 'What are your actual margins? Because bakeries are notoriously thin at 5-10%' is not — it answers itself.
